items.name: Roof Recover & Overlay in Shreveport, LA
items.meta: Roof recover and overlay systems for Shreveport-Bossier's older commercial buildings, installed over existing dry, sound roofs to cut cost and downtime versus full tear-off.

sections.html:
Recover Systems: Getting a New Roof Without Tearing Off the Old One

Recover is the strategy we reach for most in Shreveport-Bossier, given how much of this market's commercial building stock is older and still watertight enough to build on rather than demolish. Installing a new membrane over an existing roof — instead of tearing the old system off first — keeps a building dry throughout construction and generally costs less than full replacement, provided the roof underneath qualifies.

Why Recover Fits So Much of This Market

Manufacturing plants, casino properties, and medical office buildings across Shreveport-Bossier were often built with multi-ply asphalt roofs designed for a long service life through periodic maintenance. When that original roof reaches the point where patching no longer keeps up, a lot of facilities managers assume the next step is full tear-off. Often it isn't. If the deck and insulation underneath are still dry and structurally sound, recovering over that roof with a new membrane — TPO, PVC, or another cap sheet system — gets you a new roof surface at meaningfully lower cost, with far less disruption to operations below.

The Moisture Survey: The Step That Decides Everything

Every recover conversation we have starts with a moisture survey of the existing roof, usually done with infrared scanning or core sampling in representative areas. This tells us whether water has already worked into the insulation or deck. If it has, recovering over it just traps that moisture under a new membrane, where it keeps degrading the roof from the inside while looking fine on the surface. That's the scenario we're most careful to rule out before recommending recover, because a recover job that fails early costs more in the long run than an honest tear-off recommendation up front.

Code Limits on Roof Layers

Building code generally limits how many roof layers a structure can carry before a full tear-off becomes mandatory, and that limit varies depending on what's already up there. If your building has already been recovered once before, we check the layer count and code requirements before scoping another recover, since some buildings are already at their limit and need tear-off regardless of deck condition.

What a Recover Project Typically Involves

  • Moisture survey across the existing roof to confirm dry, sound substrate
  • Verification of existing roof layer count against current code limits
  • Removal of loose gravel, blistered sections, or other unstable surfacing
  • New insulation layer where slope correction or added R-value is needed
  • New membrane installation — TPO, PVC, or modified bitumen depending on building needs
  • Full detail work at drains, curbs, penetrations, and perimeter edge metal

The membrane choice depends on what's actually happening on your roof — chemical or grease exposure points toward PVC, a straightforward warehouse field usually runs TPO, and buildings already dealing with granulated cap sheet often stay in that family with modified bitumen. We can walk through TPO options in more detail depending on what your building actually needs.

Cost and Downtime Compared to Full Tear-Off

Recover generally costs less per square foot than full tear-off and replacement, since you're not paying for demolition, disposal, or the added labor of removing an existing system before installing the new one. It also keeps the building weathertight throughout the project instead of opening the deck to sky during construction, which matters for a casino floor, a production line, or patient space below that can't tolerate any exposure risk. We'll run both numbers for you — recover now versus tear-off now versus deferred tear-off after more patch repairs — so the decision is based on your building's real condition and your budget cycle, not a default assumption either way.

When Recover Isn't the Right Call

We turn down recover scopes more often than people expect. If a moisture survey shows widespread wet insulation, if the deck has structural issues, or if the building is already at its code-allowed layer limit, recommending recover anyway just sets you up for a failed roof and a wasted budget line a few years down the road. In those situations we'll walk you through what a proper tear-off actually costs and why it's the more honest answer, even though it's a bigger number upfront than the recover quote would have been.

If you're managing more than one property across Shreveport-Bossier with roofs of similar age and construction, it's also worth having each one surveyed on the same visit rather than reacting to leak calls one building at a time. That gives you a real picture of which roofs are strong recover candidates, which need tear-off soon, and which can wait — useful information for building a multi-year capital plan instead of budgeting roof work one emergency at a time.

Common Questions About Roof Recover and Overlay Systems

How do we know if our roof qualifies for recover instead of tear-off?

A moisture survey and a check of existing roof layers against code limits tells us. We do both before quoting anything.

Is recover actually cheaper than tear-off long term?

Usually cheaper up front, and often cheaper overall if the existing roof is a good candidate. We'll show you both numbers so you can decide based on your capital plan.

What happens if the moisture survey finds wet insulation?

We'll recommend tear-off for the affected areas rather than recovering over trapped moisture, even though that's a less convenient answer.

Can any membrane be used for a recover, or does it depend on the existing roof?

It depends on what's underneath and what the building needs going forward — we'll recommend TPO, PVC, or a modified bitumen cap sheet based on your specific situation.

How many times can a roof be recovered before it has to come off?

It depends on code limits for your jurisdiction and the layers already in place. We check that before scoping any additional recover.

items.name: TPO Single-Ply Roofing in Shreveport, LA
items.meta: TPO membrane roofing and recover installations for Shreveport-Bossier manufacturing, distribution, and commercial buildings, installed by crews who probe test every seam.

sections.html:
TPO Membrane Roofing for Shreveport-Bossier Facilities

TPO is the single-ply membrane we install most often on distribution buildings and recover projects across Shreveport-Bossier. It's heat-welded at the seams instead of glued or taped, it reflects summer heat off a low-slope roof, and it holds up well over the wide, open field areas typical of warehouse and manufacturing buildings out along I-20.

Why TPO Works Over Aging Built-Up Roofs

A large share of our TPO work in this market is a recover — new TPO membrane installed over insulation laid on top of an existing built-up roof, rather than tearing the old roof off. If the deck and insulation underneath are dry, this gets you a new watertight surface without the mess and downtime of demolition. We check moisture content across the existing roof first, because TPO installed over a wet substrate just seals a problem in rather than solving it.

For facilities managers working against a capital budget cycle, TPO recover is often the option that gets approved this year instead of getting pushed to next year's request.

TPO on I-20 Distribution and Warehouse Decks

The metal-deck warehouses and distribution centers along the I-20 corridor typically have large, unbroken membrane fields with fewer penetrations than an office or medical building. TPO's reflectivity keeps rooftop surface temperatures down, which matters for buildings with sensitive inventory or climate-controlled space below, and it reduces the thermal load on rooftop HVAC units working overtime through a Louisiana summer.

Wide roof fields also mean fewer seams overall relative to square footage, but the seams that exist need to be welded correctly the first time. A membrane that looks fine from the ground can be hiding a poorly bonded lap that opens up in the next wind event.

Seam Welding and Why Crew Experience Matters Here

TPO seams are hot-air welded, and welding quality is sensitive to humidity, membrane cleanliness, and how the welder handles the heat gun in changing field conditions. Shreveport's humidity swings through the year, so a crew that only works this material occasionally can produce inconsistent welds without realizing it until a probe test finds a cold spot. We probe test seams as we go rather than only at the end of a job, so problems get caught while the crew is still on the roof.

What Goes Into a TPO Recover Over Existing Roof

  • Moisture survey of the existing roof to confirm the deck and insulation are dry
  • Removal of loose gravel, blistered cap sheet, or other unstable surfacing
  • New insulation layer to restore slope and R-value where needed
  • Mechanically fastened or fully adhered TPO membrane installation
  • Heat-welded seams with probe testing along every lap
  • Detail work at drains, curbs, penetrations, and perimeter edge metal

Each of those steps gets photographed, and you get a close-out package that shows the roof at each stage — useful if you ever need to answer questions about warranty coverage down the line.

Membrane Thickness and Wind or Hail Exposure

We spec 60-mil membrane for standard commercial applications and step up to 80-mil on buildings with heavier foot traffic from rooftop mechanical service, or on properties that have taken hail damage before and need better impact resistance going forward. The thicker membrane costs more up front but resists puncture and fatigue better over a fifteen-to-twenty-year service life, which matters if your roof sees regular technician traffic for HVAC or generator maintenance.

Working Around Tenants and Operations During Installation

TPO installation involves heat welding equipment and some odor from adhesives during a fully adhered install, so we coordinate timing with sensitive tenants — a medical suite, a food service operation, a casino floor — the same way we would for any other roof system. Mechanically fastened systems generate less odor and are often the better choice when a tenant below can't tolerate any adhesive smell at all. We'll walk you through which attachment method fits your building before we write the scope.

Most TPO manufacturers offer extended warranty terms, but those warranties come with installation requirements that are easy to miss if a crew isn't paying attention — specific fastening patterns, minimum insulation thickness, and documented seam probe testing at defined intervals. We install to those specifications as standard practice, not as an upsell, because a warranty that gets denied over a missed installation detail does you no good five years into a roof's service life. If you're managing a roof as part of a larger portfolio and need warranty documentation on file for a corporate real estate group or an insurer, we provide the manufacturer paperwork alongside our own inspection photos so both records line up.

Common Questions About TPO Roofing

Is TPO a good fit for a recover over our existing built-up roof?

Usually, yes, as long as a moisture survey confirms the roof underneath is dry. If it isn't, we'll tell you before you spend money on a recover that won't hold.

How long does a TPO installation take on a typical warehouse building?

It depends on square footage, deck condition, and how many penetrations need detail work, but wide-open warehouse fields generally move faster per square foot than a roof crowded with rooftop units and skylights.

Do you offer both 60-mil and 80-mil membrane?

Yes. We'll recommend thickness based on foot traffic, hail history, and how the roof will be used after installation.

Can TPO be installed without shutting down our operation below?

In almost every case, yes. We stage material and schedule loud or odorous work around your operating hours whenever the building requires it.

What's the difference between mechanically fastened and fully adhered TPO?

Mechanically fastened systems use plates and fasteners through the membrane into the deck or insulation; fully adhered systems bond the membrane down with adhesive. We'll recommend one over the other based on wind uplift requirements and tenant sensitivity to odor during installation.

items.name: Acrylic Roof Coatings in Shreveport, LA
items.meta: Acrylic roof coating for Shreveport-Bossier metal warehouse and manufacturing roofs, with rust encapsulation and reflectivity built into every recoat.

sections.html:
Acrylic Coatings for Metal and Well-Draining Roofs

Acrylic roof coating is our go-to recommendation for extending the life of metal roofs across Shreveport-Bossier's I-20 warehouse and manufacturing stock, and for well-draining single-ply or built-up roofs that don't have chronic ponding water problems. It costs less than silicone and performs well on the roof types where standing water isn't a factor.

Where Acrylic Makes More Sense Than Silicone

Acrylic coating's one real limitation is tolerance for standing water — it can soften and degrade in areas where water sits for extended periods. On a roof with good drainage and no known low spots, that limitation doesn't matter, and acrylic gives you strong UV reflectivity and weathering protection at a lower cost than silicone. Most of the R-panel and standing seam metal roofs we recoat across this market fall into exactly that category: sloped enough to shed water, so acrylic is the more budget-friendly right answer.

Rust Encapsulation on Older Metal Roofs

A lot of the metal roofing out along I-20 and through the surrounding industrial parishes has been up for twenty or thirty years, and surface rust is common at fastener heads, cut edges, and scratched panel coating. Acrylic coating systems can encapsulate light surface rust as part of the recoat process, sealing it from further moisture exposure and stopping the corrosion cycle rather than just painting over it cosmetically. We check corrosion depth during inspection, because rust that's progressed past surface level needs panel repair or replacement before coating, not coating over it.

Reflectivity and Cooling Load on Metal Buildings

Bare or aged metal roofing absorbs a lot of solar heat through a Louisiana summer, and a reflective acrylic topcoat can cut rooftop surface temperature significantly compared to an unpainted or faded panel finish. For manufacturing and warehouse buildings running rooftop-mounted HVAC or refrigeration, that reduction translates into real, measurable relief on equipment that's otherwise working overtime through July and August heat.

What a Metal Roof Recoat Project Includes

  • Fastener inspection and replacement of any failed washers before coating
  • Pressure washing to remove chalking, dirt, and biological growth from the panel surface
  • Rust treatment and encapsulation at affected fastener heads and panel edges
  • Seam and lap sealing at panel joints and flashing transitions
  • Acrylic base coat and topcoat application at manufacturer-specified thickness

Skipping the fastener check before coating is a common shortcut that shows up as new leaks within a year or two, since a coating applied over already-loose fasteners doesn't stop the underlying backout problem.

Recoat Cycle and What to Budget For

Acrylic coatings typically need a fresh topcoat on a shorter cycle than silicone, since the material doesn't hold up quite as long under sustained UV exposure. Budgeting for that recoat cycle as routine maintenance, rather than an unplanned emergency line item, keeps the roof performing and avoids the gap where a coating has worn thin enough to let leaks start again.

Which Buildings Should Skip Acrylic Entirely

If your roof has a known low spot where water sits for more than a day after rain, acrylic is the wrong coating regardless of how much cheaper it is than silicone. We see this mistake occasionally on buildings where a previous contractor recoated with acrylic across a whole roof field without accounting for a ponding area, and the coating in that one spot fails years ahead of the rest of the field. Rather than coat over that risk, we'll either recommend silicone for the whole roof or silicone specifically at the ponding areas with acrylic elsewhere, depending on what makes sense for your budget.

The distribution and manufacturing buildings along I-20 tend to have large, unbroken metal roof fields with minimal shading, which means they take the full brunt of Louisiana summer sun for a long stretch of the day. A fresh acrylic topcoat on one of these roofs makes a visible difference in rooftop surface temperature, and for a building with rooftop-mounted refrigeration or process equipment sensitive to ambient heat, that reflectivity gain is often the most cost-effective heat-load improvement available for the roof. A metal roof that's properly maintained and recoated on this kind of schedule can go through several acrylic recoat cycles over its life before panel replacement ever becomes necessary, and each cycle is cheaper than the last major repair would have been if the coating had been allowed to fully fail first.

Common Questions About Acrylic Roof Coatings

Is acrylic a good fit for our metal warehouse roof?

In most cases, yes, provided the roof drains well and doesn't have chronic ponding. It's a cost-effective way to extend service life and improve reflectivity.

Can acrylic coating fix surface rust on our panels?

Light surface rust can be encapsulated as part of the coating process. Deeper corrosion needs panel repair before we coat over it.

Why would we choose acrylic over silicone?

Cost, mainly — acrylic is less expensive and performs well on roofs without standing water problems. Silicone is the better call where ponding is a factor.

How long does an acrylic coating typically last before recoating?

It varies with exposure and film thickness, but generally on a shorter cycle than silicone, which is worth budgeting into routine maintenance.

Does coating address fastener backout on R-panel roofs?

Not on its own — we inspect and repair fasteners as part of the prep work before coating, since a coating applied over loose fasteners won't stop the underlying problem.

items.name: Built-Up Roofing (BUR) in Shreveport, LA
items.meta: Built-up roof inspection, repair, and recover for Shreveport-Bossier's older manufacturing, downtown, and medical buildings still running original multi-ply asphalt systems.

sections.html:
Built-Up Roofing: What's Actually Under Half of Downtown Shreveport

Built-up roofing, the multi-ply asphalt-and-felt system topped with gravel or a cap sheet, is still what you'll find on a large share of the older manufacturing buildings, downtown offices, and medical properties across Shreveport-Bossier. If your building was constructed before the 1990s and nobody's mentioned a full reroof, there's a good chance BUR is still doing the work under whatever's visible on the surface.

Why BUR Has Lasted This Long on So Many Buildings

A properly installed built-up roof relies on redundancy — multiple layers of felt and asphalt mean a single failure point rarely leads to an immediate leak, since water has to work through several plies before it reaches the deck. That's part of why so many of these roofs are still functioning decades after installation, patched here and there but never fully replaced. The tradeoff is that BUR requires more maintenance attention as it ages, and gravel surfacing makes it harder to spot problem areas during a quick visual walk.

Reading a Gravel-Surfaced Roof for Real Condition

Gravel ballast protects the underlying plies from UV and hail, but it also hides blisters, splits, and worn spots from a casual inspection. We rake back gravel in representative sections during an assessment rather than just walking the surface, because a roof that looks fine under a layer of stone can have significant ply separation underneath. Displaced gravel after a wind event is also worth a call to us — exposed membrane in those bare spots degrades fast under direct sun.

Ponding Water: The Most Common BUR Complaint We Get

Low-slope BUR roofs are prone to ponding in areas where the original design slope has settled over the decades, or where a rooftop unit was added later and changed the drainage pattern around it. Standing water accelerates asphalt breakdown and adds structural load the roof wasn't designed to carry long-term. We map ponding areas during inspection and can correct them with tapered insulation as part of a recover, rather than just patching around the same low spot year after year.

Repair, Recover, or Full Tear-Off: How We Decide

  • Repair when failures are isolated and the surrounding plies are still flexible and sound
  • Recover with a new cap sheet or single-ply membrane when the deck and insulation test dry
  • Full tear-off when moisture has worked into the insulation or deck across large areas
  • Partial section replacement when only certain roof areas have failed while others hold up fine
  • Drainage correction as part of any of the above when ponding is a contributing factor

We walk you through which category your roof falls into with photos and moisture readings rather than a verbal opinion alone, so you have something concrete for whoever approves the budget.

What Multi-Ply Redundancy Means for Your Maintenance Budget

A well-maintained BUR system can outlast a lot of newer single-ply installations if it's inspected regularly and patched before small issues become large ones. The catch is that "well-maintained" means someone actually checking it every year, rather than only responding when a tenant reports a stain on the ceiling. If your roof hasn't had a real inspection in a few years, that's usually the first thing worth scheduling before any repair decision gets made.

Downtown Shreveport's Mixed Building Ages and What That Means for Inspection

Downtown Shreveport buildings have gone through decades of ownership changes, renovations, and additions, and it's common for a single roof to have sections installed at different times by different contractors — original BUR in one area, a later mod-bit patch in another, a small single-ply section over a newer addition. Treating that roof as one uniform system during inspection misses the point. We map each section separately, since each one is aging on its own timeline and may need a completely different recommendation than the section next to it.

The risk with an aging BUR roof isn't usually a sudden catastrophic failure — it's slow moisture intrusion that spreads through insulation over months before it's obvious from inside the building. By the time interior damage shows up, moisture has often traveled well beyond the original entry point, turning what could have been a modest repair into a larger recover or tear-off scope. Catching that early, through regular inspection rather than waiting for a tenant complaint, is the difference between a manageable repair bill and a much bigger one.

Common Questions About Built-Up Roofing

How do we know if our BUR roof needs repair or full replacement?

It depends on how far moisture has traveled into the system. A proper inspection with gravel pulled back in test areas gives us a real answer instead of a guess.

Can a BUR roof be recovered instead of torn off?

Often yes, as long as the deck and insulation test dry. We confirm that before recommending recover over tear-off.

Why does our roof keep collecting water in the same spot?

Usually settled slope or a drainage pattern disrupted by rooftop equipment added after original construction. Tapered insulation during a recover can correct it.

Is gravel surfacing still a good option today?

It still provides good UV and hail protection, though a cap sheet or coated surface makes future inspections easier since problems aren't hidden under stone.

How often should an older BUR roof be inspected?

At least annually, plus after any significant wind, hail, or heavy rain event, since gravel surfacing hides early warning signs from a casual look.

items.name: Edge Metal, Coping & Gutters in Shreveport, LA
items.meta: Edge metal, parapet coping, and gutter repair for Shreveport-Bossier commercial roofs, where perimeter wind uplift failures cause most interior leak calls.

sections.html:
Edge Metal and Coping Failures Are Where Most Shreveport Roof Leaks Start

The field of a roof usually holds up longer than the perimeter does, year after year, storm after storm. Wind uplift concentrates hardest at corners and edges, and that's where fasteners back out, coping shifts, and gutters separate first, well before the membrane itself shows any real wear. We treat the perimeter as its own system, not an afterthought to the field membrane.

Why the Perimeter Fails Before the Field of the Roof Does

Wind doesn't load a flat roof evenly. Uplift pressure concentrates hardest at corners, then along edges, with the field of the roof taking the least load by comparison. Every wind event flexes edge metal, coping caps, and their fasteners a little more, and over enough cycles across enough storm seasons, those connections loosen even on roofs where the membrane itself is still performing fine and shows no visible wear at all.

That's the pattern we see repeatedly across Shreveport-Bossier buildings: a roof with years of life left in the membrane, but coping caps working loose or edge metal fasteners backed out enough that the next significant wind event turns a cosmetic issue into an actual leak.

It's also the part of a roof inspection that gets skipped most often when someone's only looking at the field membrane from a distance. Coping and edge metal condition has to be checked by hand, fastener by fastener in the worst cases, because a cap can look fine from six feet away and still be one strong gust from lifting off entirely.

Edge and Gutter Components We Repair or Replace Most Often

On the calls we run for loose or failing perimeter details across office, medical, and industrial buildings alike, the same handful of components show up again and again:

  • Loose or missing coping caps on parapet walls
  • Gutter systems clogged or sagging under debris load
  • Fascia and edge metal fasteners backed out by wind cycling
  • Termination bar pulling away from the wall substrate
  • Gutter joints separated and leaking at the seams
  • Continuous cleat systems failing at edge metal joints
  • Downspouts disconnected from gutters at building corners

Gutters on Older Downtown Buildings vs. Newer Metal Buildings

Downtown Shreveport's older commercial stock mostly runs parapet walls with coping caps, where the perimeter detail is about keeping water from getting behind the cap and into the wall assembly. Newer metal buildings in the industrial parks off I-20 and I-49 more often use a straightforward gutter-and-downspout setup at the eave, where the failure mode is usually a clogged or sagging gutter rather than a coping problem.

We service both types regularly, and the fix looks different depending on which system you actually have, which is part of why a generic gutter cleaning company often misses what's actually wrong on a parapet-and-coping building versus a straightforward eave-and-downspout metal building setup.

How Edge Metal Failure Turns Into an Interior Leak Call

Water that gets behind a loose coping cap or a pulled termination bar doesn't necessarily show up right at the edge, and by the time it does, it's often traveled through insulation or wall cavity material that's now compromised well beyond the original point of entry. It tracks down inside the wall cavity and can show up as an interior stain well away from the actual failure point, which is why a tenant's leak report that seems to point at a random interior wall often traces back to a perimeter detail nobody's looked at in years.

This is part of why we don't treat a leak call and a perimeter inspection as separate problems. When we're chasing an interior stain that doesn't match up with anything obvious in the field of the roof, checking coping and edge metal is one of the first places we look, and it's a common enough answer that we build it into standard leak diagnosis rather than treating it as an afterthought.

Questions We Get About Edge Metal and Coping

How do you know if coping is loose without climbing on the roof?

You usually can't tell reliably from the ground. A hand-check of coping fasteners during a routine roof walk is the only real way to catch this before it becomes a leak, which is part of why we include it in our maintenance visits.

Can edge metal be repaired section by section, or does it need full replacement?

Section repair is often possible if the damage is localized to one wall or corner, but if fasteners are failing across most of the perimeter, a full edge metal replacement is usually the more durable and cost-effective fix in the long run.

Do gutters need cleaning as part of routine maintenance?

Yes, clogged gutters are one of the more preventable causes of water backing up under edge details, and we clear them as part of our standard maintenance visits.

What wind speed typically pulls edge metal loose here?

It varies with the age and installation quality of the edge system, but straight-line wind from a strong squall line is more than capable of loosening marginal fasteners, which is why we check them after any significant wind event.

Is edge metal failure treated differently than membrane damage in a claim?

We document edge metal and coping damage the same factual way we document membrane damage, with photos and condition notes, though how it's categorized in a claim is a conversation between you and your insurer.

items.name: Emergency Roof Tarp & Dry-In in Shreveport, LA
items.meta: Emergency tarp and dry-in response for Shreveport-Bossier buildings after storm damage, focused on stopping water intrusion and protecting tenant space fast.

sections.html:
Getting a Building Dry Fast After the Roof Opens Up

When wind tears a membrane loose or a limb comes through the deck, the first job isn't the permanent fix, it's stopping water from getting any further into the building. We show up to secure the opening, protect what's underneath, and buy time to plan the real repair without the ceiling below turning into its own problem.

These calls almost never come with advance notice, and they rarely come in one at a time during an active storm system. We keep crews and material staged specifically so a wind event that opens up multiple roofs across the area doesn't leave any one building waiting longer than it should for a response.

What We're Actually Doing in the First Few Hours

The priority sequence on any emergency call is the same regardless of cause, whether it's a wind-torn membrane, a fallen limb, or a piece of blown rooftop equipment: secure the breach against continued water intrusion, protect tenant space and equipment directly below, and document what we're seeing before we cover it up. That documentation matters later, whether for a repair scope or an insurance record, so we photograph the damage as we go rather than after the fact, before tarps and plywood cover up exactly what needs to be on record.

We're not trying to make the roof look finished on an emergency call. A tarp tied down properly and a section of exposed deck covered with plywood is the right outcome for the first visit, even if it isn't pretty. The goal is dry and stable, not permanent, and we tell you that plainly before we leave the site.

What's underneath the breach changes how we prioritize the response. A retail floor with some water on carpet is a different urgency level than a server room, an operating suite, or a casino floor with electrical equipment at stake. We ask about what's directly below before we're even on site so the crew shows up with the right approach already in mind.

What We Bring on an Emergency Call

Because these calls come in with little notice, our trucks carry what a real dry-in requires without waiting on a supply run:

  • Reinforced tarps and battens sized to the opening
  • Temporary flashing material for torn membrane edges
  • Water extraction equipment for interior standing water
  • Plywood for exposed or damaged deck sections
  • Photo documentation equipment for the insurance record
  • Sandbags and ballast for securing tarps against continued wind
  • Generator-powered tools for after-hours or power-outage calls

Coordinating With Your Building Engineer and Tenants During the Response

We check in with whoever's on site before we start work, not after, both to understand what's happening below the breach and to flag anything that looks like a life-safety concern, exposed wiring, ceiling tile sagging under water weight, that kind of thing. We keep staging out of exits and parking wherever the site allows, since an emergency response shouldn't create a second inconvenience on top of the first.

For buildings with round-the-clock operations, like a hotel, a hospital wing, or a casino floor, we coordinate directly with whoever's running the overnight shift rather than waiting for a business-hours contact. A dry-in crew working over an occupied, operating space at 2 a.m. needs someone on-site who can speak for the building in the moment, and we ask for that contact before we ever mobilize.

What Happens After the Building Is Dry

Once the immediate breach is secured and the building's dry, we walk the site with you or your property manager to talk through what a permanent repair actually looks like and roughly when it needs to happen. If the damage is limited, that might be a scheduled patch within days. If it's extensive, we hand off the documentation we've already gathered so it feeds straight into the repair scope and any insurance conversation, without starting the paperwork over from scratch.

We also use that walk-through to talk honestly about timeline. Some permanent repairs can happen within days once material is on hand; others, especially if the emergency exposed a bigger problem with an aging roof, turn into a longer conversation about full repair or replacement scope. Either way, you leave that walk-through knowing what's next instead of waiting on a callback.

Questions We Get on Emergency Calls

How fast can you get someone on site after hours?

We treat active water intrusion as a same-visit priority regardless of the hour, and response time depends mainly on how far the crew has to travel and current storm conditions across the area.

Is a tarp a permanent fix?

No, it's a temporary measure to stop water intrusion until we can scope and schedule the actual repair. We tell you upfront that it's not the end of the job.

Do you document damage while you're securing the roof?

Yes, we photograph the damage before covering it, since that record matters for both the repair scope and any insurance discussion you need to have afterward.

What if the storm knocked out power on site?

Our crews carry generator-powered tools for exactly that situation, so a power outage doesn't delay the response.

Will you work with our insurance adjuster during the emergency phase?

We can walk the roof with an adjuster if you'd like us there, and we'll answer factual questions about what we found and secured, though the claim itself is handled between you and your carrier.

items.name: EPDM Commercial Roofing in Shreveport, LA
items.meta: EPDM rubber roof repair, recover, and replacement for Shreveport-Bossier manufacturing and office buildings, with seam and flashing inspection built into every visit.

sections.html:
EPDM Rubber Roofing for Older Shreveport-Bossier Buildings

EPDM has been on Shreveport-Bossier commercial roofs for a long time, and a lot of the manufacturing and older office buildings we work on still carry an original ballasted or fully adhered EPDM system installed decades ago. Whether you need a patch, a partial section replacement, or a full tear-off and re-install, we work this material regularly enough to know exactly where it tends to fail first.

Where EPDM Actually Fails on These Roofs

Almost every EPDM failure we get called out for starts at the seams. The old seam tape used on installations from the 1990s and early 2000s loses its bond over time, especially at flashing details around HVAC curbs and parapet walls. By the time a tenant reports a ceiling stain, the seam has usually been letting water in for months, tracking laterally across the insulation before it ever shows up inside the building. We check every lap and flashing detail during an inspection, rather than only the areas near the reported leak.

Ballasted systems add another variable: displaced stone from wind events can expose membrane to UV it was never meant to see uncovered, accelerating breakdown in spots you wouldn't expect.

Patch, Partial Replace, or Full Reroof

We don't default to a full tear-off just because a roof has a few problem seams. If the membrane elsewhere is still flexible and the deck is dry, resealing and re-taping failed seams can add years of service life for a fraction of replacement cost. Where the membrane has hardened and cracked across large sections — usually from age and repeated UV exposure — a section replacement or full reroof becomes the more honest recommendation. We'll walk the whole roof with you and point out exactly which category each area falls into.

Black EPDM Versus White EPDM in This Climate

Standard black EPDM absorbs heat, which can work in its favor during Shreveport's shorter winter but adds real cooling load through the long, humid summer. White EPDM reflects more solar heat and can reduce rooftop surface temperatures noticeably, which matters for buildings with rooftop-mounted HVAC or refrigeration equipment working hard through July and August. For manufacturing buildings running process equipment that's sensitive to ambient heat, that difference is worth discussing before you commit to a color.

Ballasted, Mechanically Fastened, and Fully Adhered Systems

  • Ballasted EPDM held down with stone or pavers, common on older heavier-deck buildings
  • Mechanically fastened EPDM using plates and fasteners through the membrane
  • Fully adhered EPDM bonded with adhesive for wind-exposed or lightweight-deck buildings
  • Seam reinforcement using cover tape at high-stress laps and T-joints
  • Perimeter and curb flashing detail work, the most common repair point

We'll match the attachment method to your deck type, wind exposure, and whatever loading restrictions your building has — a ballasted system isn't an option on a roof that can't carry the extra dead load, for example.

What a Facilities Manager Should Know Before Approving Repair Work

If your roof is older EPDM and you're getting periodic leak calls, ask for a full-roof condition assessment before authorizing spot repairs. Spot repairs that chase individual leak reports without a broader look at seam condition tend to become a recurring line item on your maintenance budget, one small invoice at a time, without ever addressing the roof's actual remaining service life. A single assessment gives you a clearer number to bring to whoever approves capital spend.

Recovering an Aging EPDM Roof Instead of Full Tear-Off

EPDM doesn't always need to come off entirely once it starts showing age. If the membrane field is still generally sound and the failures are concentrated at seams and flashing, a new membrane can sometimes go down as a recover layer over the existing EPDM, provided the deck and insulation test dry underneath. That approach saves the cost and downtime of full tear-off while giving you a fresh, warrantied surface going forward.

We check this option on every older EPDM roof we inspect, because a lot of facilities managers assume replacement is the only path once a roof passes a certain age, when recover is often still on the table. A number of the manufacturing plants we service in Shreveport-Bossier have also added rooftop equipment over the years — exhaust fans, condenser units, ductwork — that wasn't part of the original EPDM installation. Each addition means new penetrations and new flashing details, and those add-on penetrations are disproportionately where we find leaks during inspection. If your building has had equipment added to the roof at any point, those spots deserve extra attention regardless of how old the rest of the membrane is.

Common Questions About EPDM Roofing

Our roof has EPDM from the 1990s — is it worth repairing or should we replace it?

It depends on membrane flexibility and how widespread the seam failures are. We'll give you a section-by-section read so you're not guessing.

Can EPDM be recovered instead of torn off?

In some cases, yes, depending on deck condition and local code limits on how many roof layers a building can carry. We'll confirm during inspection.

Does ballasted EPDM work on every building?

No — it depends on structural capacity for the added ballast weight. We check that before recommending a ballasted system.

How often should EPDM seams be inspected?

We recommend at least an annual walk, plus a check after any significant wind or hail event, since seam and flashing failures are rarely visible from the ground.

Is white EPDM worth the extra cost over black?

If you're running heat-sensitive equipment on the roof or trying to cut summer cooling costs, often yes. We can walk through the numbers for your specific building.

items.name: Insurance Documentation Support for Roof Claims in Shreveport, LA
items.meta: Factual roof damage documentation for Shreveport-Bossier insurance claims, including dated photos, moisture scans, and condition history for your adjuster.

sections.html:
Documentation That Holds Up When a Roof Claim Gets Reviewed

Our role in a roof claim is documentation, not negotiation. We put together a factual, dated record of what happened to the roof, what condition it was in before, and what a repair or replacement actually requires, and we hand that record to you and your adjuster to work with, organized in a way that's easy to follow. We don't promise outcomes on a claim we don't control, and we won't tell you what you want to hear if it isn't accurate.

Facilities managers who've been through a roof claim before, whether on this building or a prior one, usually already know the value of good, thorough documentation gathered close to the event itself. What tends to catch owners off guard is how much weight a clear, dated, technically specific record carries compared to a hurried set of phone photos taken the morning after a storm by whoever happened to be on site first, which is the gap we're built to close.

What We Document and When

If we already maintain your roof, we're working from an existing baseline of prior inspection photos and condition notes, which makes post-event comparison straightforward and considerably faster to put together. If we're coming in fresh after a storm without any prior history on file, we document current condition as thoroughly as we can and note clearly where pre-existing wear appears distinct from new, storm-related damage.

Either way, timestamps matter. We date every photo and note the date of the event you're reporting, because a claim built on documentation with a clear timeline holds together better than one with a stack of undated pictures.

We also keep the documentation specific to the exact roof system actually installed on your building, not a generic description that could apply to any commercial roof. A modified bitumen roof and a TPO membrane fail differently under wind and hail, and a documentation package that describes the damage in terms specific to your actual system reads as more credible than generic language that could describe any roof.

What Goes Into the Documentation Package We Hand Off

A complete package gives your adjuster, your property manager, and your own internal records everything needed to evaluate the damage thoroughly, without requiring a second site visit just to confirm basic facts already on record:

  • Dated, time-stamped photos of every area of damage
  • A roof plan marked with damage locations
  • Moisture scan or core sample results where taken
  • Prior inspection or maintenance history for context
  • A written description of damage, separated from pre-existing wear
  • Material and system specifications for the existing roof
  • A repair or replacement scope with itemized costs

Working Alongside Your Adjuster Without Overstepping Our Role

We're glad to walk the roof with an adjuster if you want us there, and we'll answer technical questions about the roof system, the specific damage we found, and what a proper, code-compliant repair actually involves. What we don't do is negotiate the claim itself or make any promises about how it will be evaluated or paid. That part of the process belongs to you and your carrier, and we stay in our own lane on the project, which is the roof itself, not the settlement.

We've found this distinction matters to the facilities managers and owners we work with. You need someone who can speak accurately to what happened to the roof and what fixing it requires, not someone making claims about coverage decisions that aren't ours to make. Keeping that boundary clear tends to make the whole process go smoother for everyone involved, including the adjuster.

Why Documentation Before the Storm Matters as Much as After

A claim is a lot easier to support when there's a documented baseline showing what the roof looked like before the event happened, rather than starting the comparison from memory or a vague sense of prior condition. Buildings already on one of our maintenance programs have that baseline sitting in our files already, photographed and dated across prior visits, which usually shortens how long it takes to assemble a complete post-storm package considerably. If you don't have a baseline on file, we can still document current condition thoroughly, we just note that a pre-loss baseline wasn't available.

Questions We Get About Claim Documentation

Do you help us file our claim?

We don't file the claim itself, that's between you and your carrier, but we provide the documentation and technical detail you need to support it.

Will you tell us whether our claim will be approved?

No, we don't make that call and won't promise an outcome. What we can tell you is whether the damage is real, how it happened as best we can determine, and what fixing it requires.

Do you meet with our insurance adjuster on site?

Yes, if you'd like us there, we'll walk the roof with your adjuster and answer any technical questions about the system and the damage.

What if we don't have a baseline inspection on file already?

We document current condition as thoroughly as possible and note that a pre-loss baseline wasn't available, rather than guessing at what the roof looked like before.

How long does the documentation package usually take to put together?

It depends on the size of the roof and extent of damage, but for most buildings we can turn around a complete package within a matter of days after the site visit.

items.name: Metal R-Panel Roofing in Shreveport, LA
items.meta: Metal R-panel roof repair, fastener maintenance, and recoat programs for warehouse and manufacturing buildings along the I-20 corridor and outlying Shreveport-Bossier parishes.

sections.html:
Metal R-Panel Roofing for I-20 Warehouses and Plant Buildings

R-panel is the exposed-fastener metal roofing you'll find on most of the warehouse, manufacturing, and industrial-service buildings scattered along the I-20 corridor and out through the surrounding parishes toward Minden and Mansfield. It's cost-effective to install over a large roof field, and when it's maintained properly it holds up well against Louisiana's heat and storm cycles for a long service life.

Why R-Panel Is Everywhere Out Along I-20

Distribution buildings, manufacturing plants, and the service yards supporting Haynesville Shale operations around Minden and Mansfield tend to be large-footprint, single-story buildings where cost per square foot drives the roofing decision more than appearance. Exposed-fastener R-panel gets a big roof watertight at a lower installed cost than standing seam or a full single-ply membrane system, which is why it shows up so consistently on this type of building across the outlying parishes.

Fastener Backout: The Most Common R-Panel Problem

Because R-panel fasteners drive directly through the panel face into the structural framing, they're exposed to the same thermal expansion and contraction that the panel itself goes through across the seasons. Over years of cycling, fasteners work loose — backing out slightly with each thermal cycle until the rubber washer underneath no longer seals properly. That's the single most common repair call we get on R-panel roofs: a scattering of failed fasteners letting water track along the panel underside long before it ever shows up as a visible interior leak.

Recoat and Recover Options for Aging R-Panel

Rather than tearing off and replacing an entire R-panel roof once fastener and coating wear set in, a lot of Shreveport-Bossier facilities managers get more useful life out of a recoat program: re-fastening loose panels, replacing failed washers, sealing seams, and applying an acrylic roof coating over the panel field to extend service life and improve reflectivity. For a warehouse or plant building where budget is the driving factor, that approach often makes more sense than a full panel replacement.

What a Typical R-Panel Maintenance Visit Covers

  • Fastener inspection across the field, with re-torque or replacement where washers have failed
  • Panel seam and lap condition check for gaps or lifted edges
  • Ridge cap and eave flashing inspection, common leak points on these buildings
  • Corrosion check at cut panel edges and around roof penetrations
  • Coating condition assessment if the roof has been previously recoated

Most of these buildings run wide open interior space below — warehouse racking, production floor, or equipment bays — so a small leak at a single fastener can travel a long way across a purlin before it drips somewhere visible, making it look like a bigger problem than it is at the source.

Wind Uplift and Panel Attachment in Storm Season

R-panel roofs on large, open buildings take real wind load during Shreveport-Bossier's spring severe weather season, and panels that have already lost fastener grip are at higher risk of uplift damage in a wind event. If your roof hasn't had a fastener check in a few years, doing one ahead of storm season is one of the more cost-effective maintenance calls a facilities manager can make on this roof type.

R-Panel on Haynesville Shale Service Yards and Outlying Parishes

Out through Minden, Mansfield, and the smaller communities that grew up around Haynesville Shale gas field activity, R-panel covers everything from equipment storage buildings to office and shop space serving oil-field operations. These buildings often sit further from a service crew's regular route than the ones inside Shreveport-Bossier proper, so a fastener or seam problem that goes unnoticed tends to sit longer before anyone calls it in. We recommend a standing maintenance schedule for these outlying properties rather than a reactive one, since a small leak caught during a routine visit is a lot cheaper than one that's been dripping onto stored equipment or inventory for months.

Warehouse and industrial R-panel roofs also often run long, low-slope panel fields with correspondingly long ridge caps and eave trim runs, and those are the details most likely to loosen or lift first — well before the panel field itself shows any distress. A ridge cap that's worked loose lets wind-driven rain in during a storm even when every panel underneath is sound, which is why we check these details specifically rather than assuming a clean panel field means a watertight roof.

Common Questions About R-Panel Roofing

Our R-panel roof has scattered leaks — do we need a full replacement?

Usually not. Most R-panel leaks trace back to failed fasteners or seam gaps that can be repaired without replacing the whole roof.

Is a recoat worth it instead of replacement?

For a roof with sound panels but aging fasteners and coating, yes — a recoat can add years of service life at a fraction of replacement cost.

How often should fasteners be checked on an R-panel roof?

We recommend a check every one to two years, and definitely before storm season if the roof is more than ten years old.

Can R-panel roofs be repaired without shutting down our operation?

Yes, in nearly all cases repair work happens from the roof surface without disrupting operations underneath.

Does R-panel handle Louisiana heat and storms as well as other systems?

With regular fastener maintenance and seam attention, yes — it's a proven system for this climate, which is why it's so widely used on industrial buildings here, from Shreveport-Bossier proper out to the smaller service yards along the parish lines.

items.name: Modified Bitumen Roofing in Shreveport, LA
items.meta: Modified bitumen cap sheet recover and repair for Shreveport-Bossier's older built-up roofs, torch-applied or self-adhered depending on occupancy.

sections.html:
Modified Bitumen Roofing as a Recover Over Aging Membrane

Modified bitumen shows up constantly in our recover work across Shreveport-Bossier, mostly because it lays down well over the existing gravel-surfaced built-up roofs that cover so much of the legacy manufacturing stock in this market. A granulated cap sheet installed over a properly prepped existing roof gives you a durable, walkable surface without the cost and disruption of tearing the old roof out first.

Why So Much Older Shreveport Stock Is a Good Candidate

Manufacturing buildings from the 1970s and 80s, and some of the older medical and office properties near downtown, were built with multi-ply asphalt roofs designed for a long service life with periodic maintenance rather than replacement. When those roofs reach the point where patching no longer keeps up with new leaks, modified bitumen recover is often the most cost-effective next step, because the existing insulation and deck are usually still in serviceable shape underneath the wear layer.

We confirm that with a moisture survey before quoting anything, since a recover over a wet deck just buries a problem instead of solving it.

Torch-Applied Versus Self-Adhered Systems

Torch-applied modified bitumen uses an open flame to melt the modified asphalt backing and bond the sheet to the substrate, which gives a strong, consistent bond but requires careful fire-safety protocol on an occupied building. Self-adhered systems skip the torch entirely, using a peel-and-stick backing that's a better fit for buildings where an open flame anywhere near the roof isn't acceptable — which describes most of the occupied casino and medical properties we work on. We'll recommend the application method based on your building's occupancy and any fire-watch requirements your insurer has in place.

Granulated Cap Sheet and Foot Traffic Durability

A lot of the buildings we service have rooftop mechanical equipment that gets serviced regularly, which means the roof surface takes real foot traffic year-round. Granulated cap sheet modified bitumen holds up to that kind of repeated technician traffic better than a smooth membrane, and the granule surface also adds a layer of UV protection that extends the sheet's service life through Shreveport's long, hot summers.

What a Modified Bitumen Recover Job Involves

  • Moisture survey across the existing roof to confirm dry insulation and deck
  • Removal of loose gravel and any blistered or delaminated sections
  • Base sheet or new insulation layer where slope correction is needed
  • Cap sheet installation, torch-applied or self-adhered based on building requirements
  • Flashing and detail work at all penetrations, curbs, and perimeter edge metal

Close-out documentation includes photos of the prep work and finished detail areas, which matters if you're managing this roof as one property in a larger portfolio and need a record for the file.

Ponding Water and Drainage on Older Low-Slope Roofs

A common problem on aging built-up roofs is drainage that's degraded over decades of minor deck settlement, leaving low spots where water sits for days after a storm. Modified bitumen recover gives us a chance to correct slope with tapered insulation in those areas rather than just laying a new membrane over the same drainage problem. If your roof has known ponding spots, tell us before the site walk so we can plan for the correction in the scope.

Scheduling Torch Work Around Casino and Medical Building Hours

A number of the properties where modified bitumen recover comes up in Shreveport-Bossier operate around the clock — casino floors, hospital wings, production lines that never fully shut down. Torch-applied work requires a fire watch and coordination with your building's fire alarm and suppression systems, which we set up with your facilities or life-safety staff before the crew ever lights a torch. On buildings where that coordination is more complicated than the schedule allows, self-adhered modified bitumen sidesteps the issue entirely, which is often the simpler answer for a facility that can't accommodate open-flame work windows.

Where a roof has already been recovered once with modified bitumen, matching the new cap sheet's granule color and surface type to the existing installation can also matter for both appearance and how the roof reads during future inspections — a mismatched patch is easy to mistake for a failure area from a distance. We keep track of what's already on a building when we've worked on it before, and we ask about prior roofing history on buildings we haven't, so the new work integrates cleanly with what's already there.

Common Questions About Modified Bitumen Roofing

Can modified bitumen go directly over our existing built-up roof?

In most cases, yes, provided a moisture survey confirms the deck and insulation are dry.

Is torch-applied modified bitumen safe on an occupied building?

It can be done safely with proper fire-watch protocol, but on occupied casino or medical buildings we typically recommend self-adhered systems to avoid the open flame entirely.

How long does a modified bitumen cap sheet typically last?

Service life varies with exposure and maintenance, but a properly installed and maintained system holds up well through repeated Louisiana summers when flashing details are kept in good condition.

Can this system fix our ponding water problem?

Often, yes — tapered insulation installed as part of the recover can correct drainage in known low spots.

Does modified bitumen handle rooftop mechanical foot traffic well?

Yes, granulated cap sheet is one of the more traffic-tolerant membrane surfaces available, which is part of why it's common on buildings with active rooftop equipment.

items.name: PVC Roofing in Shreveport, LA
items.meta: PVC membrane roofing built for grease, oil, and chemical exposure at Shreveport-Bossier casino kitchens, restaurants, and hospital rooftop exhaust zones.

sections.html:
PVC Roofing for Kitchens, Exhaust, and Chemical Exposure

PVC membrane comes up whenever a Shreveport-Bossier roof has to deal with grease, oil, or chemical exposure that would break down a standard TPO or EPDM system faster than it should last. Casino kitchen exhaust, restaurant strip retail, and hospital rooftop exhaust are the three situations where we recommend PVC most often in this market, because its chemistry resists the exact contaminants those roofs actually see.

Why Grease and Kitchen Exhaust Change the Membrane Decision

Restaurant and casino kitchen exhaust puts grease-laden air directly onto the roof surface downwind of the exhaust fan. Over time, that grease settles into a standard membrane and softens it, leading to premature failure right in the exhaust discharge path — often years before the rest of the roof shows any wear. PVC resists that kind of chemical attack far better than TPO or EPDM, which is why we steer casino and restaurant clients toward it even though it costs more up front than a standard single-ply.

Hospital and Medical Building Rooftop Exhaust

Medical office buildings along the Ochsner LSU Health corridor often have rooftop exhaust from lab equipment, sterilization units, or specialty HVAC that can carry chemical byproducts most membranes weren't designed to handle. If your building has any specialty exhaust on the roof, it's worth telling us before we scope a reroof — the exhaust discharge zone may need PVC even if the rest of the roof could run a less expensive membrane.

Hot-Air Welded Seams and Long-Term Seam Strength

Like TPO, PVC seams are heat-welded rather than taped or glued, but PVC's chemistry gives it a slightly different weld window and requires a crew that's used to the material's specific behavior under the heat gun. We probe every seam during installation and again at close-out, because a weld that looks bonded from the surface can still be under-fused underneath.

PVC as a Recover Option Over Existing Built-Up Roofs

Plenty of the older manufacturing and casino-adjacent buildings we work on in Shreveport-Bossier have built-up roofs from decades back that are dry enough underneath to recover rather than tear off. PVC can go down as a recover membrane over that existing roof the same way TPO can, and it's worth considering any time the building has ongoing grease, oil, or chemical exposure that would shorten a standard membrane's life anyway.

What We Check Before Recommending PVC

  • Type and source of any rooftop exhaust — kitchen, lab, mechanical, or process
  • Existing membrane condition and whether recover or tear-off makes sense
  • Deck and insulation moisture content across the field
  • Drainage pattern and whether grease or oil pools near roof drains
  • Foot traffic pattern from kitchen or mechanical service technicians

That last point matters more than people expect — kitchen exhaust systems get serviced often, and a roof near a busy exhaust unit takes more foot traffic than the rest of the field. We account for that in how we detail the membrane around those zones.

Cost Versus Standard Single-Ply

PVC runs higher than TPO for the same square footage, and we're upfront about that in every proposal. The case for it comes down to what's actually landing on your roof. If you've got grease-laden exhaust, chemical fume discharge, or a history of premature membrane failure near an exhaust unit, the extra cost usually pays for itself by avoiding a repeat reroof five years later.

The strip retail centers along Youree Drive and out near the I-20 exits mix restaurants, service businesses, and retail under a shared roof structure, which means one tenant's kitchen exhaust can affect membrane condition across a section of roof shared with neighboring units that have nothing to do with food service. When we scope a reroof for one of these multi-tenant buildings, we map out where exhaust discharge actually lands rather than assuming exposure is uniform across the field, and we'll recommend PVC only where the exposure pattern actually calls for it. That kind of targeted approach keeps costs reasonable for property owners managing a mixed-tenant roof instead of paying premium membrane pricing across square footage that never sees any grease or chemical exposure at all.

Grease and chemical exposure concentrates hardest right at curb flashings and equipment penetrations, which is exactly where a membrane transitions and becomes most vulnerable to failure. We give those details extra attention on any PVC installation — proper curb wrap, secondary seal at fastener penetrations, and enough clearance around exhaust discharge points that grease drip doesn't pool directly against a seam. Skipping this level of detail is how an otherwise good PVC installation still ends up with a localized failure right where it mattered most.

Common Questions About PVC Roofing

Do we need PVC for the whole roof or just near the kitchen exhaust?

Often just the affected zone, though on smaller roofs it can make sense to run PVC across the whole field for consistency. We'll walk the roof with you and show you the actual exposure pattern.

Is PVC worth the added cost over TPO?

If your roof sees grease, oil, or chemical exhaust, generally yes — it resists that kind of exposure far longer than TPO. If it doesn't, TPO is usually the more cost-effective choice.

Can PVC be installed as a recover over our existing roof?

In most cases, if the existing roof is dry and structurally sound, yes.

How do you handle scheduling around a live kitchen or hospital exhaust system?

We coordinate directly with your facilities or engineering team so exhaust systems stay operational, or we schedule shutdowns during the lowest-impact hours for your operation.

Does PVC come in different colors for aesthetic requirements?

Yes, PVC is available in multiple colors, which sometimes matters for visible rooftop equipment near street-facing parapets or upper-floor windows.

items.name: Roof Drains & Scuppers in Shreveport, LA
items.meta: Roof drain and scupper repair, cleaning, and capacity checks for Shreveport-Bossier commercial buildings, addressing ponding water before it becomes structural.

sections.html:
Keeping Roof Drains and Scuppers From Becoming Your Next Flood Call

Drainage is the least glamorous system on a flat roof and the one most likely to get ignored until water is standing where it shouldn't be. We treat drains and scuppers as their own maintenance category, not an afterthought to the membrane, because a roof with a perfect membrane and a clogged drain still floods.

Facilities teams tend to notice drainage only when it's already failing, water pooling somewhere it hasn't before, a ceiling stain that traces back to an overflowing scupper rather than a membrane defect. We'd rather catch a partially clogged drain on a routine visit than get the call after a heavy rain event has already found its way inside.

Internal Drains vs. Scuppers, and What's on Your Roof

Internal drains route water down through the building via interior piping run through the structure itself, while scuppers are through-wall openings that let water discharge directly off the roof edge, usually into a gutter or leader below rather than through the building's interior. A lot of older downtown Shreveport buildings run a simpler drainage layout than current code would require if they were designed today, sometimes with fewer drains serving more roof area than modern standards call for.

Knowing which system your building has, and whether it's actually adequate for the roof's current square footage, is the first question we answer before recommending anything.

Plenty of buildings run both systems together, interior drains as the primary path and scuppers as overflow protection if the interior drains ever get overwhelmed or clogged during a heavy rain event. Knowing which role each opening plays on your specific roof matters, because a scupper that's been mistaken for decorative or unused can end up sealed off by a past repair, removing the exact backup protection it was there for.

What We Check on Every Drain and Scupper Visit

Drainage problems are usually preventable long before they cause an interior flood call, which is why this is one of the more consistent, unglamorous parts of any maintenance visit we run:

  • Drain bowl and strainer basket clear of debris
  • Overflow scupper height set correctly against the roof deck
  • Drain leader lines clear down to grade or the storm system
  • Scupper flashing and through-wall seal intact
  • Ponding depth and duration checked after a rain event
  • Drain count adequate for the roof's current square footage
  • Secondary or overflow drainage present as required by code

Ponding Water: When It's Cosmetic and When It's a Structural Problem

A little standing water right after a hard rain is normal on most flat roofs and clears within a day once drains are functioning properly and the roof has any reasonable slope to it. Water still standing 48 hours after rainfall stops is a different situation, and it's worth taking seriously, both because standing membranes degrade faster under constant moisture exposure and because sustained ponding adds real weight that a deck wasn't necessarily designed to carry indefinitely, especially on an older building.

We track ponding location and duration across visits so we can tell you whether it's a one-time drainage hiccup or a recurring pattern that points to a slope or drain-capacity problem underneath.

The heaviest rain events in this region, whether from a slow-moving spring system or the tail end of a Gulf storm pushing up along I-20, are exactly when marginal drainage gets exposed. A roof that drains fine during routine rain can still pond for days after a multi-inch event if the drain count or slope was never quite adequate to begin with.

Undersized Drainage on Older Buildings

Buildings built to older code standards sometimes have fewer drains than current code would require for the same roof area, and that gap becomes obvious during the heaviest rain events this region sees, particularly when a slow-moving spring system or a tropical remnant drops several inches faster than an undersized system was ever designed to move it. Adding drainage capacity without a full reroof is possible in some cases, but often the more durable fix is a tapered insulation retrofit during a future reroof project, which lets us correct slope and drainage at the same time instead of treating them separately.

Questions We Get About Drains and Scuppers

How often should drains be cleaned?

At minimum twice a year as part of routine maintenance, with an extra check after any storm heavy enough to wash significant debris onto the roof.

How do we know if we have enough drains for our roof size?

We calculate required drainage capacity against your roof's actual square footage and compare it to what's currently installed, which tells us quickly whether the building is under-drained by current standards.

What causes recurring ponding in the same spot?

Usually a low spot in the original roof slope, a partially clogged drain nearby, or insulation that's settled unevenly over time. We map recurring ponding locations to figure out which of those it is.

Do scuppers need maintenance if we already have interior drains?

Yes, scuppers usually function as overflow protection even on roofs with primary interior drains, and a blocked or damaged scupper removes that backup exactly when you need it most.

Can drainage capacity be added without a full reroof?

Sometimes, depending on the roof's construction, but a tapered insulation retrofit during a future reroof is often the more complete and lasting fix for chronic drainage shortfalls.

items.meta: Silicone roof coating and restoration for Shreveport-Bossier's aging BUR, modified bitumen, and metal roofs, built to handle standing water and long UV exposure.

sections.html:
Silicone Coatings: Buying Years Back on an Aging Roof

Silicone roof coating is the tool we reach for most often when a Shreveport-Bossier facilities manager needs to extend a roof's life without the capital hit of a full tear-off. Applied over existing BUR, modified bitumen, single-ply, or metal roofs, silicone restores a watertight, reflective surface and can push a full reroof decision several years further down your capital plan.

Why Restoration Beats Replacement on So Much of This Market's Stock

Given how much of Shreveport-Bossier's older commercial building stock is still running roofs installed decades ago, coating rather than replacing is often the more realistic answer for a facilities budget. A silicone coating system, properly applied over a roof that's still structurally sound, can add ten to fifteen years of service life at a fraction of full replacement cost — and it keeps the building watertight throughout the process instead of exposing the deck during tear-off.

We're upfront that coating isn't the right call on every roof. If the deck or insulation is already wet, or the membrane has failed structurally rather than just cosmetically, coating over it just delays a bigger problem. That's why every coating quote starts with an inspection and moisture survey, not a sales pitch.

Ponding Water Resistance — Where Silicone Beats Other Coatings

Silicone's biggest advantage over acrylic coating is its tolerance for standing water. A lot of the older low-slope BUR and modified bitumen roofs across this market have known low spots where water sits for a day or more after a storm, and acrylic coatings can soften or degrade in those exact areas over time. Silicone holds up under sustained ponding, which makes it the right choice for roofs with drainage issues that aren't getting corrected as part of the same project. See our recover and overlay page if drainage correction needs to happen alongside the coating.

UV Reflectivity and Rooftop Heat Load

Shreveport summers put real UV and heat stress on any roof surface, and a fresh silicone coating reflects a significant amount of that solar load back off the building instead of absorbing it into the membrane. For manufacturing buildings running process equipment sensitive to ambient heat, or any building with rooftop HVAC working overtime through July and August, that reflectivity translates into a real reduction in rooftop surface temperature and, often, a noticeable easing on cooling equipment.

What Goes Into a Silicone Recoat Project

  • Full roof inspection and moisture survey to confirm the substrate is sound and dry
  • Pressure washing and surface prep to remove chalking, debris, and biological growth
  • Detail work at seams, flashings, and penetrations before base coat application
  • Base coat and topcoat application at manufacturer-specified thickness
  • Granule or embedded fabric reinforcement at high-stress seams and details where needed

Skipping the prep steps is where a lot of coating jobs fail early — silicone bonds to a clean, dry, properly prepared surface, not to whatever chalking or contamination has built up on the roof over the years.

Recoat Cycles and Maintaining Your Investment

A silicone coating isn't a one-time fix and forget — it's a maintained system that gets a fresh recoat on its own cycle, typically every ten to twenty years depending on exposure and film thickness. Facilities managers who track this on a maintenance calendar, rather than waiting for visible failure, get more consistent life out of the system and avoid the surprise of finding a fully degraded coating during storm season.

Applying Coating on Occupied Casino and Medical Buildings

Silicone coating application generates far less odor and disruption than a torch-down or fully adhered membrane installation, which makes it one of the more tenant-friendly ways to extend a roof's life on a building that can't tolerate a lot of downtime or fumes. Casino floors that run continuously and medical buildings with sensitive HVAC intake both benefit from a restoration approach that doesn't require the same level of operational disruption as a full reroof. We still coordinate timing around your building's needs, but coating work generally has a lighter footprint on the operation below than most other roofing projects.

That said, coating restores and extends a sound roof — it doesn't correct a structurally compromised deck, fix widespread wet insulation, or replace a membrane that's already failed in large sections. We'll tell you directly if your roof has moved past the point where coating makes sense, rather than selling a coating job that won't hold up and leaves you back at square one within a couple of years.

Common Questions About Silicone Roof Coatings

Can silicone coating go over our existing built-up or modified bitumen roof?

In most cases, yes, provided the substrate is dry and structurally sound. We confirm that with an inspection before quoting.

Is silicone coating a permanent fix or a temporary patch?

It's a real restoration system, not a stopgap — properly applied and maintained, it can add a decade or more of service life.

Why would we choose silicone over acrylic coating?

Silicone tolerates standing water far better than acrylic, which matters if your roof has any known low spots or drainage issues.

Does coating work on metal roofs too?

Yes, silicone can be applied over sound metal roofing to extend its service life and improve reflectivity, similar to how acrylic is used on R-panel roofs.

How do we know if our roof is a good candidate for coating instead of replacement?

An inspection and moisture survey will tell us. If the substrate is dry and sound, coating is usually on the table.
